reasoning:
SLB has experienced a significant downtrend over the past quarter, dropping from around $40 to the $32–$33 range. This is largely driven by a bleak macroeconomic outlook for oil, with global investment banks downgrading crude forecasts to $56–$68 and OPEC+ beginning to unwind production cuts (adding ~411k bpd each month starting June 2025). The most recent close on June 6 is $33.14, a one-day spike up from $32.36, which puts it at the highest level of the last two weeks (averaging ~$32.50). 

Given the yfinance tool's downward linear model (estimating P > 33.14 at 0.46) and the high likelihood of short-term mean reversion after a recent jump, the near-term probability leans slightly negative. However, the stock is fundamentally cheap (EV/EBITDA ~6.3x) and management is returning a minimum of $4B to shareholders in 2025 (dividends and buybacks). Because these positive basic valuations will gradually offset short-term bearishness over time, and because uncertainty must increase toward 0.5 at farther horizons per instructions, the long-term forecasts converge directly to 0.50.
